funding_source: No external funding. Internal research project.
citi_training_completion: Principal Investigator has completed CITI training in Human Subjects Research (Social/Behavioral Research).
protocol_description: This exploratory study examines how people interested in management topics — including business students and working employees (e.g., professionals, managers, executives) — rate the effectiveness of common workplace / HR tactics in situational judgment scenarios. We do not assume group differences in advance; analyses explore whether and how ratings vary across self-described roles (e.g., student, working professional, executive). Primary data collection occurs as an in-class course assignment in MNGT 425 and MNGT 502; community participants interested in management topics are also welcome. Aggregate findings may be shared to support student–community engagement (e.g., helping students learn from and connect with working professionals around management topics). There is no deception and no formal debriefing.
population_description:
Adults 18 years of age or older who are interested in management topics, including:
(1) Business students at Nicholls State University, primarily those enrolled in MNGT 425 (HR Analytics) and MNGT 502, for whom participation is offered as an in-class course assignment for credit; and
(2) Working employees (any employed adults, preferably those interested in management topics — e.g., professionals, managers, executives), recruited through community / professional outreach.

Classroom structure (MNGT 425 / MNGT 502): Students are asked to participate in this study as a course assignment. An equivalent alternative assignment is available: write a qualitative description of how one would respond to each scenario prompt and submit it to the instructor for course credit. Alternative-assignment submissions are used only for grading; they are not included in the main research dataset and are not analyzed as study data at this time (no qualitative analysis pipeline yet). No recruitment flyer is used for students. Participation in research (Option A) versus the alternative (Option B) is the student's choice; both satisfy the assignment for credit.
research_procedures:
The study involves the following procedures:

1. Informed Consent: Participants read and provide electronic consent before research tasks. MNGT 425 / MNGT 502 students who prefer not to participate in research complete the alternative assignment for the instructor instead; those submissions are not research data and are not analyzed as part of this study.

2. Session identifier: An opaque session identifier is generated for the research record. Names are not stored with research ratings. There are no PRAMS ID numbers for this study.

3. Brief background (optional/minimal): Participants may indicate role category (e.g., student, working professional, executive) for exploratory comparisons. No sensitive identifiers are required.

4. HR Situational Judgment Test (HR SJT): Participants complete the online HR SJT (27 scenarios). For each scenario, they may rate tactic effectiveness on a 1–5 scale. Ratings are optional; each situation includes Skip (no forced responding).

5. Optional decision notes: At the end of each situation, participants may optionally enter free-text notes about how they are thinking through that decision (e.g., for the instructor). Notes are not required.

6. Feedback: Participants may receive a feedback report summarizing their ratings. There is no deception and no formal debriefing procedure.

7. Emails (separate; not formal study data): Optional email addresses may be collected on a separate lab form (not on the SJT survey) so the lab can share aggregate findings and updates about HSIRB-approved studies. Emails are not linked to research ratings as part of formal data collection.

Approximate duration: 45–60 minutes. Data collection is online via university-hosted systems.
research_objectives:
This is an exploratory study. Objectives include:
1. Describe how participants rate the effectiveness of HR/management tactics across 27 situational judgment scenarios.
2. Explore whether rating patterns differ across self-described groups (e.g., business students, working professionals, executives) without assuming differences a priori.
3. Identify which tactics tend to be rated most and least effective across HR domains.
4. Support teaching in MNGT 425 and MNGT 502 through an in-class assignment structure.
5. Share aggregate findings (no individuals identified) to support community engagement and help students learn from / connect with working professionals around management topics.
research_questions:
1. How do participants rate the effectiveness of management/HR tactics across scenarios?
2. Exploratory: Do rating patterns differ across role groups (e.g., students, working professionals, executives)?
3. Which tactics are rated most and least effective within and across HR domains?
4. What aggregate patterns may be useful to share with students and community participants for learning and networking around management topics?
educational_justification:
This research serves educational purposes by:
- Providing students with hands-on experience in HR situational judgment assessment
- Teaching students about evidence-based HR management and its measurement
- Demonstrating research methodology and data collection procedures
- Contributing to students' understanding of HR decision-making in organizational contexts
recruitment_method:
No recruitment flyer is used for the course assignment.

(1) MNGT 425 and MNGT 502 students: The instructor announces the study as an in-class course assignment via normal classroom / LMS communication. Students may (a) participate in the research study by completing the online HR SJT after informed consent, or (b) complete an equivalent qualitative alternative assignment submitted to the instructor for credit. Alternative submissions are grading-only and not analyzed as research data.

(2) Working employees / community participants interested in management topics: Invited through professional and community outreach (e.g., networks, email) using a brief study description and consent link. No flyer required.

(3) Lab studies list (separate form): A public page lists HSIRB-approved studies available for participation and offers an optional email signup to stay informed about lab studies. That form is not part of formal SJT data collection.
recruitment_script:
MNGT 425 / MNGT 502 — Classroom Assignment Announcement (verbatim; no flyer)

Study Title: HR Situational Judgment Test: Evidence-Based HR Decision-Making

As an in-class assignment, you are asked to participate in an exploratory research study on how people interested in management topics rate the effectiveness of workplace tactics.

Option A — Research study assignment:
Complete the online HR Situational Judgment Test (27 scenarios). You may rate tactic effectiveness (optional; you may skip any situation) and optionally add notes about how you are thinking through each decision. After informed consent, your ratings may be included in the research dataset. Duration: approximately 45–60 minutes. Must be 18 or older.

Option B — Alternative assignment (not research / not analyzed as study data):
Write a qualitative description of how you would respond to each scenario prompt and submit it to the instructor for equivalent course credit. This is used only for grading.

Both options satisfy the course assignment.

---

Community / working employees — outreach description (verbatim):

Study Title: HR Situational Judgment Test: Evidence-Based HR Decision-Making

You are invited to complete an online situational judgment exercise with 27 workplace scenarios. We welcome working employees and others interested in management topics. Participation is voluntary, takes about 45–60 minutes, and is for adults 18+. Aggregate findings may later be shared with participants who optionally join a separate lab mailing list.
inclusion_criteria:
- Must be at least 18 years of age
- Interested in management topics (preferred)
- For course-credit path: currently enrolled in MNGT 425 or MNGT 502 at Nicholls and choose Option A (research assignment)
- For community path: working employee or other adult interested in management topics
- Access to a computer/device with internet; able to complete a web-based assessment
exclusion_criteria:
- Under 18 years of age
- Unable to complete the online assessment due to technical limitations
- Previously participated in this study (duplicate responses)
- Chose the qualitative alternative assignment (Option B) — those submissions are not research data and are not analyzed as part of this study
benefits_to_subjects:
Participants may benefit from:
- Completing an in-class assignment for course credit (MNGT 425 / MNGT 502; Option A or equivalent Option B)
- Optional feedback report on their ratings
- Contributing to exploratory understanding of how different groups view management/HR tactics
- Optionally receiving aggregate findings via a separate email form (not part of formal data collection), which may support learning and community networking around management topics
benefits_to_others: Students and community members may benefit from shared aggregate insights that support learning and networking around management topics. Educators may use patterns to improve instruction.
benefits_to_society: Exploratory evidence on how students and working adults evaluate management/HR tactics; stronger university–community engagement around evidence-based management.
payment_compensation: No monetary compensation. MNGT 425 and MNGT 502 students earn equivalent assignment credit for Option A (research) or Option B (qualitative alternative to the instructor). Option B is not research data and is not analyzed as study data. Community participants are uncompensated volunteers. Optional feedback reports may be provided.
costs_to_subjects: Time only (~45–60 minutes for the SJT). No financial cost. Option B is a written alternative for course credit only.
review_type_justification:
This study qualifies for exempt status under Category D because:

1. Minimal risk (online situational judgment ratings and optional notes)
2. Research data are recorded with opaque session identifiers only — no PRAMS ID numbers; names and emails are not stored with research ratings
3. Standard educational / survey procedures; no deception; no formal debriefing required
4. Adults 18+; no targeted vulnerable populations
5. Optional emails for aggregate updates are collected on a separate form and are not part of formal SJT data collection

Techniques: (1) educational tests / cognitive SJT; (3) survey procedures.
risk_statement: Minimal risk. Possible mild boredom or fatigue; optional feedback may differ from expectations. Privacy risk is minimal because research ratings use opaque session identifiers (no PRAMS IDs; no names/emails with ratings). Risks are no greater than ordinary coursework or online surveys.
risk_mitigation:
1. Equivalent alternative assignment for MNGT 425 / MNGT 502 (Option B; grading only; not analyzed as research).
2. Opaque session identifiers only for research ratings — no PRAMS IDs; names/emails not stored with ratings.
3. Optional ratings, Skip, optional notes; withdraw anytime without penalty.
4. No deception; no formal debriefing.
5. Optional emails collected only on a separate lab form, not linked as formal study data.
6. Secure university-hosted storage; access limited to PI / authorized personnel.
data_collection_methods:
Research data (formal collection):
1. Opaque session identifier (no PRAMS ID numbers; names not stored with ratings).
2. Optional role category for exploratory group comparisons.
3. HR SJT ratings (optional) and optional per-situation decision notes.
4. Session metadata (e.g., start/end, duration).

Not formal research data:
- MNGT 425 / MNGT 502 Option B qualitative submissions (instructor grading only; not analyzed).
- Optional emails on the separate lab studies / stay-informed form (to share aggregate findings and list HSIRB-approved studies).
data_storage: Research ratings and notes are stored on password-protected Nicholls-hosted systems with opaque session identifiers only (no PRAMS IDs; no names/emails with the rating dataset). Optional lab mailing-list emails are stored separately from research ratings.
confidentiality_procedures:
1. Research dataset uses opaque session identifiers only — no PRAMS ID numbers.
2. Names and emails are not stored with research ratings.
3. Optional emails for updates/findings are collected on a separate form and stored separately from formal study data.
4. Publications and shared community reports use aggregate, non-identifying summaries only.
5. Access limited to the PI and authorized research personnel.
data_retention: Research data will be retained for a minimum of 5 years after study completion, as required by IRB regulations. After this period, data may be retained longer for potential follow-up analyses or publication purposes, but will continue to be stored securely and confidentially. Anonymized data/analyses will be made available to the Open Science Framework.
data_access:
Access to research data is restricted to:
- Principal Investigator (Dr. Christopher Castille)
- Authorized research team members (if any)
- IRB members for review purposes (if requested)

Data will be accessed only for research analysis, publication preparation, and IRB compliance. All data access is logged and monitored.
consent_procedures:
Electronic informed consent before research tasks. Course students choosing Option A use the student consent flow; community participants use the community/professional consent flow. Option B students do not enter research consent; their work is grading-only.

Consent covers purpose (exploratory), procedures, voluntary/optional responding, risks, benefits, course-credit options, right to withdraw, and HSIRB contacts. No deception; no formal debriefing. Optional lab email signup is described as separate from the survey.
data_monitoring_plan: Data will be monitored throughout the study period to ensure data quality, appropriate participant recruitment, and compliance with IRB protocols. The principal investigator will review data collection progress and address any issues that arise.
oversight_procedures: Study oversight will be provided by the Principal Investigator and IRB. Any adverse events or protocol deviations will be reported to the IRB immediately.
publication_plan: Results from this study may be prepared for publication. All publications will use aggregated, anonymized data only.
data_sharing_plan: Individual participant data will be shared but in anonymized form. Aggregated data may be shared through publication and open science repositories.
participant_access_to_results: Participants who are interested in learning about the study results can contact the principal investigator to request a summary of findings.
appendices_notes: Professional/community consent; student consent (425/502 assignment + alternative); instrument (27 situations + optional notes); classroom announcement (no flyer); CITI; lab studies / stay-informed page (separate from formal data collection).
irb_contact_notes: For questions or concerns about this research, participants can contact the Principal Investigator or IRB Office (Dr. Alaina Daigle, 985-448-4697).
